titleStmt.title: Meissner State in Classical Ideal Charged Fluid

analytic.title: Meissner State in Classical Ideal Charged Fluid

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: It is shown that a model of a superconductor as a classical ideal charged fluid at constant uniform temperature in a uniform compensating positively charged background exhibits the Meissner state with the correct value of the penetration depth. The differences between this model and the textbook classical model that fails to predict the Meissner state are discussed, as are the similarities of this model to the coupled Schrödinger-Maxwell model, which does predict flux exclusion. In particular, this model does not neglect the pressure term and terms that are bilinear in the velocity in the fluid equations; these terms are essential to the results.

div.head: Introduction
div.p: It is a widely held belief that the Meissner state, the exclusion of magnetic flux by a (type I) superconductor, cannot be derived from a classical treatment of collissionless charge carriers, but instead requires a separate ansatz, such as that made by F. and H. London [1] or by Ginsburg and Landau [2]. Here, we show that the Meissner state actually results directly from a model of a superconductor as a classical ideal charged fluid at constant uniform temperature in a uniform compensating positively charged background.

In what follows, in Sec. II we develop the model, in Sec. III we discuss its gauge transformation properties, in Secs. IV and V we contrast the model with the classical textbook model and the Schrödinger-Maxwell model, and in Sec. VI we offer a brief conclusion and discussion.

div.head: Classical Ideal Charged Fluid Model
div.p: The fluid equations for this model are (cgs units)
div.formula: m ∂v v v ∂t + v v v • ∇v v v = -n -1 ∇p + q(E E E + c -1 v v v × B B B) ,(2.1a)
div.p: where (n, v) are the fluid (number density, velocity) fields, (q, m) are the (charge, mass) of the fluid particles, p is the fluid pressure, and (E E E, B) B) B) are the self-consistent (electric, magnetic) fields that satisfy the Maxwell equations (Gaussian units)
div.formula: ∇ × E E E = -c -1 ∂B B B ∂t , ∇ × B B B = c -1 ∂E E E ∂t + 4πqnv v v . (2.2b)
div.p: Here, n 0 is the space-time average of the number density n(r r r, t). Note that in (2.1a), the usual phenomenological collision term, (-mνv v v), is omitted from the right-hand side, in accord with this fluid model in which the collision frequency ν = 0. We write the electromagnetic fields in terms of their potentials in the usual way,
div.formula: E E E = -c -1 ∂A A A ∂t -∇φ, B B B = ∇ × A A A ,(2.3)
div.p: and also define the "canonical momentum field " P P P by
div.formula: P P P = mv v v + qc -1 A A A . (2.4)
div.p: Substituting these in (2.1a), we get exactly
We recall that, for uniform constant temperature, we may write

where µ(n) is the chemical potential; i.e., n -1 ∇p is a gradient. Using this in (2.5), we see that the canonical momentum field P P P may always be chosen to be a gradient. Thus we get from (2.3) and (2.4)
div.formula: ∇ × v v v = - qB B B mc ,(2.7)
div.p: and from (2.5) and (2.6)

We expect that |n -n 0 | n 0 for most situations 1 , because charge separations in a charged fluid are very difficult to manintain, and also because (2.2a) and (2.8) show that |n -n 0 | ∝ v 2 , which should be small. Therefore, for such situations, it should be a very good approximation to replace n by n 0 in (2.2b), whereby, combining (2.2) and (2.7), we get immediately
div.formula: B B B -λ -2 p B B B = 0 ,(2.9a)
div.p: where
div.formula: λ p ≡ mc 2 4πn 0 q 2 1 2 (2.9b)
div.p: is the penetration length. The static limit of (2.9) is the same as the result of the London theory.

div.head: Gauge Properties of the Classical Model
div.p: Since P P P is a gradient, we may write (2.4) as
div.formula: P P P = ∇J = mv v v + qc -1 A A A ,(3.1)
div.p: where J has the dimension of angular momentum. This result suggests that we make a gauge transformation in an attempt to eliminate J if it should happen to be non-zero: We let
div.formula: A A A = A A A + cq -1 ∇J , φ = φ -q -1 ∂J ∂t ,(3.2)
div.p: and for convenience require the Lorentz gauge for the primed potentials, which yields
The Maxwell equations (2.2) and (2.1b), (2.3), (2.4), (2.8) in this gauge then reduce exactly to
div.formula: v v v = - qA A A mc ,(3.4e)
B B B = ∇ × A A A , E E E = -c -1 ∂A A A ∂t -∇φ . (3.4g)
div.p: Note that not only does J disappear from (3.4g), but also from the fluid motion equation (2.8), which converts to (3.4c). So we may choose this gauge in simply connected regions. Note also that, in the static limit, these equations imply that n = n 0 and φ = constant, but that A A A , v v v and B B B still go to zero inside the fluid, within a short distance from the surface, unless n 0 → 0. However, in general by Stokes' theorem we must have
div.formula: C A A A • dl l l = Φ ,(3.5)
div.p: where Φ is the total magnetic flux enclosed by the arbitrary closed path C. Consider a non-simply connected region of the fluid. In particular, imagine a cylindrical annulus of ideal fluid whose thickness is much greater than λ p , and let there be a magnetic field directed along the axis inside the inner cylinder (vacuum), so that Φ = 0 for a closed path in this fluid. For example, this is the situation for an infinitely long currentcarrying circular solenoid in vacuum, surrounded by a superconductor. If we solve the static limit of (3.4a) to (3.4g) for such a case, we find that both v v v and A A A go to zero deep within the fluid. Therefore this A A A is inadequate: Although it yields the correct B B B inside the fluid, it cannot satisfy (3.5). We must add to it a term cq -1 ∇J, as in (3.2), where J = (qΦ/2πc)θ, and θ is the azimuthal angle in cylindrical coordinates. This will satisfy (3.5) without influencing the results of (3.4). Thus, we see that, even classically, J cannot be transformed away in such situations. 2

div.head: Comparison with Classical Model
div.p: The model developed above is fundamentally different from the classical models discussed in many texts [4,5,6,7], which treat the mobile charged particles as noninteracting and having uniform number density, and which also omit the v v v × B B B term in the Lorentz force and of course omit the v v v • ∇v v v term in the convective derivative, since they don't use a fluid treatment. As we have seen, the collective behavior implied by the fluid equations is essential to our results; the v v v ×B B B, v v v •∇v v v, and pressure term are all of central importance. In fact, if one regards these terms and the pressure term, which are O(v 2 ), as negligibly small, and drops them a priori, then (2.1a) reduces to ∂v v v/∂t = E E E/m. Then, from the Maxwell equations (2.2b) one gets only the time derivative of (2.7) and then only the equation ( -λ -2 p )∂B B B/∂t = 0, satisfied by any B B B that is independent of time. This is the textbook dilemma.

div.head: Comparison with Quantum Model
div.p: Perhaps the simplest quantum mechanical model is that given by Feymann [8]. He starts with the Schrödinger equation
div.formula: i ∂ψ ∂t = 1 2m -i ∇ -qc -1 A A A 2 ψ + qφψ ,(5.1)
div.p: and assumes that the particles being described are Cooper pairs that behave as bosons. Thus, all of them may be in the same (ground) state, whereby the (charge, current) densities (ρ, j j j) are given by
div.formula: ρ = q|ψ| 2 , j j j = q 2m ψ * (-i ∇ -qc -1 A A A)ψ + c.c.(5.2)
div.p: He then shows that the coupled Schrödinger-Maxwell equations are equivalent to the ideal charged fluid equations which are strikingly similar to (2.1a) and (2.2a).
In fact, if in our motion equation (2.8) we replace the chemical potential µ(n) by

, then our equations are equivalent to the hydrodynamical form of the Schödinger-Maxwell equations, from which the Meissner effect does result. 3 Curiously, the derivation of the Schrödinger equation from classical statistics [9] yields exactly Feynman's equation for a collection of charged bosons all in the ground state.

div.head: Discussion and Conclusion
div.p: We have shown that a completely classical treatment of an ideal charged fluid does predict exclusion of static magnetic fields. The crucial element of the proof lies in showing that the canonical momentum field P P P defined in (2.4) is a gradient, even in a classical treatment.

If a conventional collision term (-mνv v v) with collision frequency ν > 0 is included on the right-hand side of Eq. (2.1a), then P P P cannot be a gradient or be identically zero (see Appendix A), and then v v v = 0 rather than P P P = 0 is the classical static limit result in simply connected regions.

Note that our approximate expression (2.9) and the exact epression (3.4a) imply that, for frequencies greater than the plasma frequency ω p ≡ λ p /c, electromagnetic waves can propagate in the fluid, just as in collisionless plasma.

However; our model has several limitations. For example, it does not account for the full Meissner effect: the exclusion of the magnetic flux during the superconducting phase transition. During such a dynamical process the system is not at a uniform constant temperature, whereby (2.6) would not be applicable. In this case, the canonical momentum field P P P could not be chosen as a gradient field. Nonetheless, this classical model suitably describes the Meissner state wherein a superconductor has no internal magnetic field.

Finally, we note that neither this nor any other classical treatment can offer an explanation of the absence of electron-lattice interaction in the superconducting state; for that, one needs the insight and power of quantum mechanics and the BCS theory concept of Cooper pairs [10] or some other mechanism that provides an energy gap between the electronic ground state and excited states.

note.p: This might not be true if n 0 → 0, as should happen near a superconducting-normal transition.

In quantum mechanics, we know that J is proportional to the phase of the superconducting wavefunction, or J = l θ, where l is an integer and θ is the azimuthal angle in cylindrical coordinates. This leads to quantization of the enclosed flux, Φ = (hc/q)l. Also we know that this behaviour is the origin of the Aharonov-Bohm effect. For a fuller discussion, see[3].

Complete equivalence of the solutions of our classical equations with those of Schrödinger-Maxwell equations would require more, namely, that the function ψ ≡ n 1 2 exp(iJ/ ) be single-valued and satisfy the usual boundary conditions. In the Schrödinger-Maxwell treatment, the canonical momentum field is a priori a gradient.

div.head: Acknowledgments
div.p: We thank James M. Wilkes and Richard L. Ingraham for helpful discussions.

div.head: A Inclusion of Collision Term
div.p: If we decide to include the collision term -mνv v v on the right hand side of (2.1a) then we obtain instead of (2.5),

For a uniform constant temperature, n -1 ∇p is a gradient field as shown in (2.6). When ν = 0, the canonical momentum field P P P can be chosen to be a gradient field without loss in generality. If the collision term is nonzero but small, say |mνv v v| 1, suppose then that the canonical momentum is a gradient field plus a small correction term (r r r, t), where | (r r r, t)| 1, then we obtain instead of (2.7) and (2.8)
In the approximation where |n -n 0 | ∝ v 2 1, whereupon n can be replaced with n 0 in (2.2b), we obtain by collecting (2.2) and (A.2),

where λ p is the penetration length given in (2.9b). Therefore, only in the limit where the collision term vanishes identically do we actually recover the result of London theory in the static limit of (A.4).

analytic.title: The Electromagnetic Equations of the Supraconductor
Theorem on the reality of the electromagnetic potentials
Statistical Description of Nonrelativistic Classical Systems
